questions.title: GO Term Prediction as Multi-Label Classification
questions.description: Set up a multi-label classification model for Gene Ontology (GO) term prediction. Given protein embeddings, predict which GO terms apply using binary cross-entropy loss.
questions.info: Gene Ontology (GO) provides a structured vocabulary for annotating protein functions across three ontologies: Molecular Function (MF), Biological Process (BP), and Cellular Component (CC). Predicting GO terms is inherently a multi-label classification problem since a single protein can be annotated with multiple GO terms simultaneously. DeepGO (Kulmanov et al., Bioinformatics 2018) demonstrated that deep learning models taking sequence and interaction network features as input can effectively predict GO terms, outperforming traditional BLAST-based methods. The standard loss function for multi-label GO prediction is Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE), applied independently to each GO term output. Reference: Kulmanov et al., 'DeepGO: predicting protein functions from sequence and interactions using a deep ontology-aware classifier', Bioinformatics 2018; Radivojac et al., 'A large-scale evaluation of computational protein function prediction', Nature Methods 2013.

blanks.hint: Multi-label classification uses sigmoid (not softmax) to independently predict each label.
Binary Cross-Entropy loss is used for multi-label classification where each label is independent.
BCE loss requires float targets, not integer labels.

questions.title: Fmax Metric for GO Prediction Evaluation
questions.description: Implement the Fmax metric used in CAFA challenges to evaluate GO term predictions. Fmax is the maximum F1-score over all possible thresholds.
questions.info: The Fmax (maximum F-measure) metric is the primary evaluation metric used in the Critical Assessment of Functional Annotation (CAFA) challenges. It computes precision and recall at each threshold t in [0, 1], then takes the maximum F1-score across all thresholds. This protein-centric evaluation averages precision and recall over all proteins before combining them. CAFA (Radivojac et al., Nature Methods 2013) established this as the standard for benchmarking GO prediction methods because it accounts for the hierarchical and multi-label nature of GO annotations. The metric is threshold-independent, making it robust to different score calibrations across methods. Reference: Radivojac et al., 'A large-scale evaluation of computational protein function prediction', Nature Methods 2013; Zhou et al., 'The CAFA challenge reports improved protein function prediction and new functional annotations for hundreds of genes through experimental screens', Genome Biology 2019.

blanks.hint: Compare scores against the current threshold t from the loop.
Sum the predicted positive labels for protein i.
F1 = 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall).

questions.title: Enzyme Classification (EC Number Prediction)
questions.description: Build a hierarchical enzyme classification model that predicts Enzyme Commission (EC) numbers from protein sequence embeddings.
questions.info: Enzyme Commission (EC) numbers provide a hierarchical four-level classification system for enzymes (e.g., EC 2.7.11.1 for protein kinases). Predicting EC numbers from protein sequences is a core task in computational biology. DeepEC (Ryu et al., PNAS 2019) used convolutional neural networks for high-accuracy EC prediction from raw sequences. ProteInfer (Sanderson et al., Cell Systems 2023) later showed that dilated convolutional models trained on UniProt can predict both EC numbers and GO terms with competitive accuracy. Since EC classification is hierarchical, the top-level class (1-7) identifies the general reaction type, and multi-class cross-entropy is appropriate for this first level. Reference: Sanderson et al., 'ProteInfer, deep neural networks for protein functional inference', Cell Systems 2023; Ryu et al., 'Deep learning enables high-quality and high-throughput prediction of enzyme commission numbers', PNAS 2019.

            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.2),
            nn.Linear(256, {{b1}})
        )

questions.title: Protein-Protein Interaction Prediction
questions.description: Implement a siamese network for predicting protein-protein interactions (PPI) from paired embeddings.
questions.info: Protein-protein interaction (PPI) prediction is crucial for understanding cellular networks and signaling pathways. Modern approaches encode each protein independently with a pretrained language model, then combine the embeddings to predict whether the pair interacts. D-SCRIPT (Sledzieski et al., Cell Systems 2021) used pretrained protein embeddings with a projection module for cross-species PPI prediction. The TAPE benchmark (Rao et al., NeurIPS 2019) established standardized evaluation tasks including contact prediction, which relates to physical interactions between residues. A common architecture uses a siamese encoder that processes both proteins through shared weights, then combines representations via element-wise product or concatenation for binary classification. Reference: Sledzieski et al., 'D-SCRIPT translates genome to phenome with sequence-based, structure-aware, genome-scale predictions of protein-protein interactions', Cell Systems 2021; Rao et al., 'Evaluating protein transfer learning with TAPE', NeurIPS 2019.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class PPIPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=1280, hidden_dim=256):
        super().__init__()
        # Shared encoder for both proteins
        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(embed_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.LayerNorm(hidden_dim)
        )
        # Classifier on combined representation

            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 64),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(64, 1)
        )

        h_a = self.encoder(emb_a)
        h_b = self.encoder(emb_b)
        # Element-wise product captures interaction
        combined = {{b1}}
        return self.classifier(combined)

criterion = {{b2}}

emb_b = torch.randn(16, 1280)

questions.title: Binding Site Prediction (Per-Residue Classification)
questions.description: Implement a per-residue binding site predictor using 1D convolutions over per-residue embeddings from a protein language model.
questions.info: Binding site prediction identifies which residues in a protein interact with ligands, DNA, or other proteins. This is formulated as a per-residue binary classification task where each residue is labeled as binding or non-binding. ScanNet (Tubiana et al., Nature Methods 2022) used geometric deep learning on protein structures for binding site prediction. For sequence-based approaches, per-residue embeddings from protein language models like ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023) are fed into convolutional or recurrent layers to capture local sequence context. The key challenge is class imbalance, as binding residues are typically a small fraction of the protein. Evaluation uses per-residue AUROC and AUPRC metrics. Reference: Tubiana et al., 'ScanNet: an interpretable geometric deep learning model for structure-based protein binding site prediction', Nature Methods 2022; Lin et al., 'Evolutionary-scale prediction of atomic-level protein structure with a language model', Science 2023.

import torch.nn as nn

class BindingSitePredictor(nn.Module):

            nn.ReLU(),

            nn.Conv1d(hidden_dim, hidden_dim, kernel_size=5, padding=2),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden_dim),
        )
        self.output = nn.Conv1d(hidden_dim, 1, kernel_size=1)

        logits = self.output(h)  # (batch, 1, seq_len)
        return logits.squeeze(1)  # (batch, seq_len)

# Use pos_weight to handle class imbalance (few binding residues)

binding_labels = torch.zeros(8, 100)

loss = criterion(logits, {{b3}})

questions.title: Spearman Correlation for Fitness Prediction
questions.description: Evaluate a fitness prediction model using Spearman rank correlation, the standard metric for assessing variant effect predictions against deep mutational scanning (DMS) data.
questions.info: Spearman rank correlation is the primary metric for evaluating protein fitness predictions against experimental deep mutational scanning (DMS) data. Unlike Pearson correlation, Spearman correlation measures the monotonic relationship between predicted and measured fitness values, making it robust to nonlinear score transformations. ProteinGym (Notin et al., NeurIPS 2023) established a comprehensive benchmark of 217 DMS assays and uses Spearman correlation as the main ranking metric for comparing mutation effect predictors. The FLIP benchmark (Dallago et al., NeurIPS 2021 Datasets) also adopted Spearman correlation for evaluating fitness landscape predictions across multiple protein families. High Spearman correlation (>0.5) indicates the model captures relative fitness ordering well. Reference: Notin et al., 'ProteinGym: Large-Scale Benchmarks for Protein Fitness Prediction and Design', NeurIPS 2023; Dallago et al., 'FLIP: Benchmark tasks in fitness landscape inference for proteins', NeurIPS 2021 Datasets and Benchmarks.

from scipy import stats
import numpy as np

# Simulated DMS experimental fitness values and model predictions
np.random.seed(42)
n_variants = 500
true_fitness = np.random.randn(n_variants)
predicted_scores = 0.6 * true_fitness + 0.4 * np.random.randn(n_variants)

# Compute Spearman rank correlation

print(f"P-value: {pvalue:.2e}")
print(f"Significant: {pvalue < {{b2}}}")

# Also compute for comparison

print(f"Pearson r: {pearson_r:.4f}")

questions.title: AUROC and AUPRC for Function Classification
questions.description: Compute AUROC and AUPRC metrics to evaluate a binary protein function classifier, handling the class imbalance typical in GO annotation.
questions.info: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) and Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) are standard metrics for evaluating binary classifiers in protein function prediction. AUROC measures how well the model ranks positive examples above negative ones, while AUPRC is more informative when classes are imbalanced, as is common in GO term prediction where most proteins lack a given annotation. The CAFA challenges (Radivojac et al., Nature Methods 2013) use both term-centric AUROC and protein-centric Fmax. AUPRC is preferred over AUROC in highly imbalanced settings because AUROC can be misleadingly high when the negative class dominates. The TAPE benchmark (Rao et al., NeurIPS 2019) also employs these metrics for evaluating downstream tasks. Reference: Radivojac et al., 'A large-scale evaluation of computational protein function prediction', Nature Methods 2013; Rao et al., 'Evaluating protein transfer learning with TAPE', NeurIPS 2019.

questions.title: Fitness Landscape from DMS Data
questions.description: Build a supervised model to predict protein fitness from one-hot encoded mutations using deep mutational scanning (DMS) data.
questions.info: Deep mutational scanning (DMS) experiments measure the functional effect of thousands of single amino acid substitutions in a protein, generating fitness landscape data. The FLIP benchmark (Dallago et al., NeurIPS 2021 Datasets) provides curated DMS datasets across multiple protein families including AAV, GFP, and thermostability assays, with standardized train/test splits. ProteinGym (Notin et al., NeurIPS 2023) aggregates 217 DMS assays spanning diverse functions and organisms for benchmarking mutation effect predictors. Supervised models trained on DMS data typically take mutation features as input and regress onto the measured fitness score. The simplest encoding represents each mutation as a one-hot vector over the sequence position and amino acid identity. Reference: Dallago et al., 'FLIP: Benchmark tasks in fitness landscape inference for proteins', NeurIPS 2021 Datasets and Benchmarks; Notin et al., 'ProteinGym: Large-Scale Benchmarks for Protein Fitness Prediction and Design', NeurIPS 2023.

            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(64, {{b1}})  # scalar fitness
        )

questions.title: Zero-Shot Mutation Effect Prediction
questions.description: Implement zero-shot mutation effect scoring using masked language model log-likelihood ratios, following the ESM approach.
questions.info: Zero-shot mutation effect prediction uses pretrained protein language models to score mutations without any task-specific training data. The approach computes the log-likelihood ratio between the mutant and wildtype amino acids at the mutated position using the masked language modeling objective. ESM-1v (Meier et al., NeurIPS 2021) showed that this simple approach achieves state-of-the-art correlation with DMS experiments, and the ProteinGym benchmark (Notin et al., NeurIPS 2023) confirmed that ESM-based zero-shot scoring remains highly competitive. The score is computed as log P(mutant_aa | context) - log P(wildtype_aa | context), where the context is the surrounding sequence with the target position masked. Positive scores indicate the mutation is predicted to be beneficial. Reference: Meier et al., 'Language models enable zero-shot prediction of the effects of mutations on protein function', NeurIPS 2021; Notin et al., 'ProteinGym: Large-Scale Benchmarks for Protein Fitness Prediction and Design', NeurIPS 2023.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import numpy as np

def zero_shot_mutation_score(log_probs, position, wildtype_idx, mutant_idx):
    """Score a mutation using masked LM log-likelihood ratio.
    log_probs: (seq_len, vocab_size) log-probabilities from masked LM
    position: int, 0-indexed mutation position
    wildtype_idx: int, vocab index of wildtype amino acid
    mutant_idx: int, vocab index of mutant amino acid
    Returns: float, mutation effect score
    """
    log_p_mut = log_probs[position, {{b1}}]
    log_p_wt = log_probs[position, {{b2}}]
    return (log_p_mut - log_p_wt).item()

# Simulate masked LM output
torch.manual_seed(42)
seq_len, vocab_size = 100, 33  # 20 AAs + special tokens
logits = torch.randn(seq_len, vocab_size)
log_probs = {{b3}}(logits, dim=-1)

# Score mutation at position 50: Ala(5) -> Val(10)
score = zero_shot_mutation_score(log_probs, position=50,
                                  wildtype_idx=5, mutant_idx=10)
print(f"Mutation effect score: {score:.4f}")
print(f"Interpretation: {'Beneficial' if score > 0 else 'Deleterious'}")

Index into the log-probability vector using the wildtype amino acid index.

questions.title: Stability Prediction (ddG Regression)

questions.info: Protein stability prediction estimates the change in Gibbs free energy of folding (ddG) caused by a mutation. Negative ddG values indicate stabilizing mutations, while positive values indicate destabilizing ones. ThermoMPNN (Dieckhaus et al., 2024) and other structure-aware models combine structural features with sequence information for ddG prediction. The FLIP benchmark (Dallago et al., NeurIPS 2021 Datasets) includes thermostability prediction tasks from the Meltome dataset. ProTherm (Kumar et al., NAR 2006) provides experimental ddG measurements for training. A common approach concatenates the wildtype protein embedding with a mutation feature vector encoding the position and amino acid change, then regresses onto the experimental ddG value. Reference: Dallago et al., 'FLIP: Benchmark tasks in fitness landscape inference for proteins', NeurIPS 2021 Datasets and Benchmarks; Dieckhaus et al., 'Transfer learning to leverage larger datasets for improved prediction of protein stability changes', 2024.

questions.title: GO Hierarchy-Aware Loss Function
questions.description: Implement a hierarchy-aware loss for GO prediction that enforces parent-child consistency in the Gene Ontology DAG.
questions.info: The Gene Ontology is structured as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where child terms imply their parent terms (true-path rule). A protein annotated with 'kinase activity' must also be annotated with 'transferase activity'. DeepGOPlus (Kulmanov & Hoehndorf, Bioinformatics 2020) enforced this hierarchy by propagating predictions upward through the GO DAG. The CAFA challenges (Radivojac et al., Nature Methods 2013) require predictions to satisfy the true-path rule for valid evaluation. Hierarchy-aware losses add a penalty when a child term has higher probability than its parent, encouraging ontologically consistent predictions. This can be implemented as a max-margin constraint: for each parent-child pair, the parent score should be at least as high as the child score. Reference: Kulmanov & Hoehndorf, 'DeepGOPlus: improved protein function prediction from sequence', Bioinformatics 2020; Radivojac et al., 'A large-scale evaluation of computational protein function prediction', Nature Methods 2013.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def hierarchy_aware_loss(predictions, targets, parent_child_pairs, alpha=0.1):
    """BCE loss + hierarchy consistency penalty.
    predictions: (batch, num_terms) predicted probabilities
    targets: (batch, num_terms) binary labels
    parent_child_pairs: list of (parent_idx, child_idx) tuples
    """
    # Standard BCE loss
    bce_loss = nn.functional.binary_cross_entropy(
        predictions, targets, reduction='mean'
    )

    # Hierarchy penalty: parent should have >= child probability
    hierarchy_penalty = 0.0
    for parent_idx, child_idx in parent_child_pairs:
        parent_prob = predictions[:, parent_idx]
        child_prob = predictions[:, child_idx]
        # Penalize when child_prob > parent_prob
        violation = {{b1}}
        hierarchy_penalty += violation.mean()

    if len(parent_child_pairs) > 0:
        hierarchy_penalty /= {{b2}}

    total_loss = bce_loss + alpha * hierarchy_penalty
    return {{b3}}

# Example with 10 GO terms and 3 parent-child relationships
predictions = torch.sigmoid(torch.randn(16, 10))
targets = torch.zeros(16, 10).bernoulli_(0.1)
pairs = [(0, 3), (0, 5), (1, 7)]  # term 0 is parent of 3 and 5, etc.

loss = hierarchy_aware_loss(predictions, targets, pairs)
print(f"Hierarchy-aware loss: {loss.item():.4f}")

questions.title: Remote Homology Detection (TAPE Benchmark)
questions.description: Set up a protein fold classification model for remote homology detection using the TAPE benchmark task structure.
questions.info: Remote homology detection classifies proteins into fold categories based on structural similarity, even when sequence similarity is low. The TAPE benchmark (Rao et al., NeurIPS 2019) includes remote homology detection as one of five supervised tasks for evaluating protein representations, with 1195 fold classes. In this task, models must generalize across superfamily boundaries: test set proteins share no superfamily membership with training proteins. This tests whether learned representations capture structural information beyond simple sequence similarity. Pretrained protein language models like ESM (Rives et al., PNAS 2021) significantly improved performance on this task compared to alignment-based methods, demonstrating that self-supervised pretraining captures structural features. Reference: Rao et al., 'Evaluating protein transfer learning with TAPE', NeurIPS 2019; Rives et al., 'Biological structure and function emerge from scaling unsupervised learning to 250 million protein sequences', PNAS 2021.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class RemoteHomologyClassifier(nn.Module):
    """Classify proteins into fold classes (TAPE benchmark)."""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=1280, num_folds=1195):
        super().__init__()
        self.pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool1d(1)  # pool over sequence
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(embed_dim, 512),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.5),
            nn.Linear(512, num_folds)
        )

    def forward(self, per_residue_emb):
        # per_residue_emb: (batch, seq_len, embed_dim)
        x = per_residue_emb.transpose(1, 2)  # (batch, embed, seq_len)
        pooled = self.pool(x).{{b1}}  # (batch, embed_dim)
        return self.classifier(pooled)

model = RemoteHomologyClassifier()
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

# Variable-length sequences padded to max_len=256
emb = torch.randn(8, 256, 1280)
fold_labels = torch.randint(0, 1195, (8,))

logits = model(emb)
loss = criterion(logits, fold_labels)

# Accuracy computation
predictions = torch.{{b2}}(logits, dim=-1)
accuracy = (predictions == fold_labels).{{b3}}().item()
print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy:.4f}")

Use torch.argmax to get the index of the highest logit for each sample.

questions.title: ProteinGym-Style DMS Benchmark Evaluation
questions.description: Implement evaluation code for scoring mutation effect predictors across multiple DMS assays, following the ProteinGym protocol.
questions.info: ProteinGym (Notin et al., NeurIPS 2023) is a large-scale benchmark comprising 217 deep mutational scanning assays for standardized evaluation of mutation effect predictors. The benchmark reports per-assay Spearman correlation between predicted scores and experimental measurements, then aggregates results using the mean across all assays. This approach prevents bias toward assays with more variants. The benchmark evaluates both zero-shot methods (using pretrained models without DMS training data) and supervised methods (trained on partial DMS data). Results showed that combining evolutionary and structure-based information yields the best predictions, with methods like Tranception and ESM-1v performing well in the zero-shot setting. Reference: Notin et al., 'ProteinGym: Large-Scale Benchmarks for Protein Fitness Prediction and Design', NeurIPS 2023; Meier et al., 'Language models enable zero-shot prediction of the effects of mutations on protein function', NeurIPS 2021.

questions.title: FLIP Benchmark Data Splitting Strategy
questions.description: Implement the train/test splitting strategies from the FLIP benchmark including random, mutant-based, and position-based splits.
questions.info: The FLIP benchmark (Dallago et al., NeurIPS 2021 Datasets) introduced multiple data splitting strategies to test different generalization capabilities of fitness prediction models. Random splits test interpolation ability, while position-based splits test generalization to unseen mutation positions. The mutant-count split groups variants by the number of simultaneous mutations, testing extrapolation from single to multiple mutations. These splits reveal that many models achieve high random-split performance but fail to generalize across positions or mutation depths, highlighting the gap between interpolation and extrapolation in protein fitness prediction. FLIP covers proteins including AAV capsid, GFP fluorescence, GB1 binding, and thermostability datasets. Reference: Dallago et al., 'FLIP: Benchmark tasks in fitness landscape inference for proteins', NeurIPS 2021 Datasets and Benchmarks.
questions.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

def flip_random_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42):
    """Standard random split."""
    return train_test_split(X, y, test_size=test_size,
                           random_state=random_state)

questions.title: Per-Term AUROC for Multi-Label GO Prediction
questions.description: Compute per-GO-term AUROC scores and macro-averaged AUROC for multi-label protein function prediction.
questions.info: In multi-label GO term prediction, evaluation must account for the independent nature of each GO term prediction. The term-centric evaluation computes AUROC for each GO term separately, then averages across terms. The CAFA challenges (Radivojac et al., Nature Methods 2013) use both term-centric and protein-centric evaluation views. DeepGO (Kulmanov et al., Bioinformatics 2018) reported per-ontology AUROC scores across MF, BP, and CC sub-ontologies. A key consideration is that GO terms with very few positive examples may have unreliable AUROC estimates, so terms with fewer than a minimum number of positive annotations are often excluded from the macro-average. import numpy as np

def per_term_auroc(y_true, y_scores, min_positive=5):
    """Compute per-GO-term AUROC with minimum support filter.
    y_true: (n_proteins, n_terms) binary
    y_scores: (n_proteins, n_terms) predicted probabilities

            term_aurocs.append(auc)

    return term_aurocs, macro_auroc

questions.title: GNN-Based Protein Interaction Network Prediction
questions.description: Implement a graph neural network layer for predicting interactions in a protein-protein interaction network using message passing.
questions.info: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are naturally suited for protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks where proteins are nodes and interactions are edges. DeepGO (Kulmanov et al., Bioinformatics 2018) incorporated protein interaction network features alongside sequence information for GO prediction, demonstrating that network context improves function prediction. GNN-based approaches propagate information along the PPI graph, allowing a protein's predicted function to be informed by its interaction partners. Message passing neural networks (MPNNs) iteratively update node representations by aggregating features from neighboring nodes. The node update rule combines the current node embedding with aggregated neighbor messages. Link prediction in PPI networks is typically formulated by scoring pairs of node embeddings with a dot product or learned classifier. Reference: Kulmanov et al., 'DeepGO: predicting protein functions from sequence and interactions using a deep ontology-aware classifier', Bioinformatics 2018; Zitnik & Leskovec, 'Predicting multicellular function through multi-layer tissue networks', Bioinformatics 2017.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class PPIGraphConv(nn.Module):
    """Simple graph convolution for PPI networks."""
    def __init__(self, in_dim, out_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.linear = nn.Linear(in_dim, out_dim)
        self.activation = nn.ReLU()

    def forward(self, node_features, adj_matrix):
        # adj_matrix: (n_nodes, n_nodes) normalized adjacency
        # Aggregate neighbor features
        aggregated = {{b1}}  # message passing
        out = self.linear(aggregated)
        return self.activation(out)

    def __init__(self, embed_dim=1280, hidden_dim=256):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = PPIGraphConv(embed_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.conv2 = PPIGraphConv(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)

    def forward(self, node_features, adj_matrix, edge_pairs):
        h = self.conv1(node_features, adj_matrix)
        h = self.conv2(h, adj_matrix)
        # Score edges by dot product of node embeddings
        src, dst = edge_pairs[:, 0], edge_pairs[:, 1]
        scores = (h[src] * h[dst]).{{b2}}
        return scores

adj = adj / adj.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True)

questions.title: Smin Metric for Ontology-Aware Evaluation (CAFA)
questions.description: Implement the Smin (minimum semantic distance) metric used in CAFA alongside Fmax for ontology-aware evaluation of GO predictions.
questions.info: The Smin metric, used alongside Fmax in CAFA evaluations, measures the minimum semantic distance between predicted and true GO annotation sets across all thresholds. It is based on information content (IC) of GO terms, weighting more specific (rarer) terms higher than general ones. The information content of a GO term is calculated as the negative log of its annotation frequency: IC(t) = -log2(freq(t)). Smin combines remaining uncertainty (ru) -- IC of true terms not predicted -- and misinformation (mi) -- IC of predicted terms that are not true. The CAFA challenge (Radivojac et al., Nature Methods 2013) uses Smin to complement Fmax because it accounts for the specificity of GO terms, penalizing more for missing or hallucinating specific functional annotations. Lower Smin indicates better predictions. Reference: Radivojac et al., 'A large-scale evaluation of computational protein function prediction', Nature Methods 2013; Clark & Radivojac, 'Information-theoretic evaluation of predicted ontological annotations', Bioinformatics 2013.

import numpy as np

def compute_smin(y_true, y_scores, ic_values, thresholds=None):
    """Compute Smin: minimum semantic distance over thresholds.
    y_true: (n_proteins, n_terms) binary annotations
    y_scores: (n_proteins, n_terms) predicted probabilities
    ic_values: (n_terms,) information content per GO term
    """
    if thresholds is None:
        thresholds = np.arange(0.01, 1.0, 0.01)

    best_smin = float('inf')
    for t in thresholds:
        y_pred = (y_scores >= t).astype(int)

        ru_list, mi_list = [], []
        for i in range(y_true.shape[0]):
            # Remaining uncertainty: IC of true terms NOT predicted
            fn_terms = (y_true[i] == 1) & (y_pred[i] == 0)
            ru = np.sum(ic_values[{{b1}}])

            # Misinformation: IC of predicted terms that are NOT true
            fp_terms = (y_true[i] == 0) & (y_pred[i] == 1)
            mi = np.sum(ic_values[fp_terms])

            ru_list.append(ru)
            mi_list.append(mi)

        avg_ru = np.mean(ru_list)
        avg_mi = np.mean(mi_list)
        smin = np.sqrt(avg_ru**2 + {{b2}})
        best_smin = {{b3}}

    return best_smin

# Example: 100 proteins, 50 GO terms
np.random.seed(42)
y_true = np.random.binomial(1, 0.1, (100, 50))
y_scores = np.random.beta(2, 5, (100, 50))
y_scores[y_true == 1] += 0.3
y_scores = np.clip(y_scores, 0, 1)

# IC values: rarer terms have higher IC
term_freq = np.random.uniform(0.01, 0.5, 50)
ic_values = -np.log2(term_freq)

smin = compute_smin(y_true, y_scores, ic_values)
print(f"Smin: {smin:.4f}")

Smin = sqrt(ru^2 + mi^2), so square the average misinformation term.

questions.title: Dilated Convolutions for Function Prediction (ProteInfer-Style)
questions.description: Implement dilated convolutional layers as used in ProteInfer for predicting protein functions directly from amino acid sequences.
questions.info: ProteInfer (Sanderson et al., Cell Systems 2023) uses dilated convolutional neural networks to predict protein functions (GO terms and EC numbers) directly from amino acid sequences. Dilated convolutions expand the receptive field exponentially without increasing the number of parameters, allowing the model to capture long-range sequence dependencies important for function prediction. ProteInfer stacks multiple convolutional blocks with exponentially increasing dilation rates (1, 2, 4, 8, ...), similar to WaveNet architecture. The model was trained on UniProt annotations and achieved competitive performance with methods using pretrained language model embeddings, demonstrating that task-specific architectures can be effective. Global average pooling converts variable-length sequence representations to fixed-size vectors for classification. Reference: Sanderson et al., 'ProteInfer, deep neural networks for protein functional inference', Cell Systems 2023.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class DilatedBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, channels, kernel_size=3, dilation=1):
        super().__init__()
        padding = dilation * (kernel_size - 1) // 2  # same padding
        self.conv = nn.Conv1d(channels, channels,
                              kernel_size=kernel_size,
                              dilation={{b1}},
                              padding=padding)
        self.norm = nn.BatchNorm1d(channels)
        self.activation = nn.ReLU()

    def forward(self, x):
        residual = x
        out = self.activation(self.norm(self.conv(x)))
        return out + residual  # residual connection

class ProteInferStyle(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size=21, embed_dim=128, num_terms=500):
        super().__init__()
        self.embedding = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)
        # Dilated conv stack with increasing dilation rates
        self.conv_stack = nn.Sequential(
            DilatedBlock(embed_dim, dilation=1),
            DilatedBlock(embed_dim, dilation=2),
            DilatedBlock(embed_dim, dilation=4),
            DilatedBlock(embed_dim, dilation=8),
            DilatedBlock(embed_dim, dilation=16),
        )
        self.pool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool1d({{b2}})  # global average pool
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(embed_dim, num_terms)

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (batch, seq_len) token indices
        h = self.embedding(x).transpose(1, 2)  # (batch, embed, seq_len)
        h = self.conv_stack(h)
        h = self.pool(h).squeeze(-1)  # (batch, embed_dim)
        return {{b3}}  # (batch, num_terms)

model = ProteInferStyle()
seqs = torch.randint(0, 21, (8, 300))  # batch of 8, length 300
logits = model(seqs)
print(f"Output shape: {logits.shape}")

Global average pooling reduces the sequence dimension to 1.

        # Task-specific heads
        self.heads = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.Linear(hidden, 1) for _ in range({{b1}})])

    def forward(self, x):
        shared_repr = self.shared(x)
        outputs = [head(shared_repr) for head in self.heads]
        return torch.cat(outputs, dim={{b2}})

model = MultiTaskADMET()
X = torch.randn(16, 2048)
y = torch.randn(16, 5)
mask = torch.randint(0, 2, (16, 5)).float()  # missing label mask

pred = model(X)
# Masked MSE loss: only compute loss for available labels
loss = (((pred - y) ** 2) * mask).sum() / mask.{{b3}}().clamp(min=1)
print(f'Masked loss: {loss.item():.4f}')

questions.title: Compound-Protein Interaction with Cross-Attention
questions.description: Implement a cross-attention mechanism for compound-protein interaction (CPI) prediction where drug tokens attend to protein residues.
questions.info: Compound-protein interaction (CPI) prediction determines whether a small molecule binds to a target protein. Cross-attention mechanisms allow drug atom representations to attend to relevant protein residues, learning interaction-specific features. DrugBAN (Bai et al., ICML 2022) uses bilinear attention to model pairwise interactions between drug substructures and protein subsequences. The BioSNAP dataset and BindingDB provide large-scale CPI benchmarks. Attention-based models offer interpretability by highlighting which drug-protein atom-residue pairs contribute most to the interaction prediction. This approach outperforms simple concatenation of drug and protein embeddings. Reference: Bai et al., 'Interpretable bilinear attention network with domain adaptation improves drug-target prediction', ICML 2022; Tsubaki et al., 'Compound-protein interaction prediction with end-to-end learning of neural networks for graphs and sequences', Bioinformatics 2019.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class CrossAttentionCPI(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, drug_dim=128, prot_dim=128, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(drug_dim, hidden)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(prot_dim, hidden)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(prot_dim, hidden)
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)

    def forward(self, drug_tokens, prot_tokens):
        # drug_tokens: (batch, n_atoms, drug_dim)
        # prot_tokens: (batch, n_residues, prot_dim)
        Q = self.W_q(drug_tokens)
        K = self.W_k(prot_tokens)
        V = self.W_v(prot_tokens)
        scale = Q.size(-1) ** {{b1}}
        attn = torch.bmm(Q, K.transpose(1, 2)) / scale
        attn_weights = F.softmax(attn, dim={{b2}})
        context = torch.bmm(attn_weights, V)  # (batch, n_atoms, hidden)
        pooled = context.mean(dim=1)
        return self.classifier(pooled).squeeze(-1), attn_weights

model = CrossAttentionCPI()
drug = torch.randn(8, 20, 128)  # 20 atoms
prot = torch.randn(8, 100, 128)  # 100 residues
scores, attn = model(drug, prot)
loss = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()(scores, torch.{{b3}}(8))
print(f'Scores shape: {scores.shape}, Attn shape: {attn.shape}')

blanks.hint: Scaled dot-product attention divides by the square root of key dimension, i.e., d^0.5.
Apply softmax over the last dimension (key/residue dimension) to normalize attention weights.
Generate random binary labels for the batch using randint or rand().round().

questions.info: Protein solubility prediction is critical for recombinant protein production and biotherapeutic development. Sequence-derived features such as amino acid composition, charge distribution, and hydrophobicity are strong predictors of solubility. The SOLpro method (Magnan et al., Bioinformatics 2009) was among the first ML approaches, using SVMs on sequence features. The eSOL dataset (Niwa et al., PNAS 2009) provides experimental E. coli cell-free solubility measurements for thousands of proteins. Gradient boosting classifiers (e.g., XGBoost) perform well on tabular solubility features. DeepSol (Khurana et al., Bioinformatics 2018) later showed CNNs on raw sequences can match or exceed feature-based methods. Reference: Magnan et al., 'SOLpro: accurate sequence-based prediction of protein solubility', Bioinformatics 2009; Khurana et al., 'DeepSol: a deep learning framework for sequence-based protein solubility prediction', Bioinformatics 2018.

questions.title: Protein Aggregation Propensity Prediction
questions.description: Build a 1D-CNN model to predict aggregation-prone regions in protein sequences from residue-level features.
questions.info: Protein aggregation is a major concern in biotherapeutic development and is linked to neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's. Aggregation-prone regions (APRs) are short hydrophobic stretches that can form beta-sheet structures. TANGO (Fernandez-Escamilla et al., Nature Biotech 2004) pioneered computational APR prediction using statistical mechanics models. Aggrescan (Conchillo-Sole et al., BMC Bioinformatics 2007) uses an amino acid aggregation propensity scale derived from experimental data. Deep learning approaches frame APR prediction as residue-level binary classification with 1D convolutions over sequence windows. The AmyLoad database provides experimentally validated amyloidogenic peptide sequences for training and evaluation. Reference: Fernandez-Escamilla et al., 'Prediction of sequence-dependent and mutational effects on the aggregation of peptides and proteins', Nature Biotech 2004; Conchillo-Sole et al., 'AGGRESCAN: a server for the prediction of aggregation-prone regions in protein sequences', BMC Bioinformatics 2007.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class AggregationPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=20, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv1d(input_dim, hidden, kernel_size={{b1}}, padding=3)
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv1d(hidden, hidden, kernel_size=5, padding=2)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (batch, seq_len, input_dim) one-hot encoded
        x = x.transpose(1, 2)  # (batch, input_dim, seq_len)
        h = self.relu(self.conv1(x))
        h = self.relu(self.conv2(h))
        h = h.{{b2}}(1, 2)  # back to (batch, seq_len, hidden)
        return self.fc(h).squeeze(-1)  # per-residue scores

model = AggregationPredictor()
seq_onehot = torch.randn(4, 150, 20)  # batch of 4, length 150
labels = torch.randint(0, 2, (4, 150)).float()

pred = model(seq_onehot)
loss = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()(pred, labels)
loss.backward()
print(f'Output shape: {pred.shape}, Loss: {loss.{{b3}}():.4f}')

questions.title: Peptide Immunogenicity Prediction
questions.description: Train a neural network to predict whether a peptide will elicit a T-cell immune response based on peptide sequence encoding and MHC context.
questions.info: Immunogenicity prediction estimates whether a peptide-MHC complex will activate T cells, a critical step beyond MHC binding prediction. The IEDB database (Vita et al., Nucleic Acids Res 2019) provides curated T-cell assay data for training immunogenicity models. Unlike MHC binding prediction, immunogenicity depends on T-cell receptor recognition, antigen processing, and peptide stability in the MHC groove. DeepImmuno (Li et al., Brief Bioinform 2021) uses deep learning on peptide sequences combined with HLA type information. Amino acid encoding schemes like BLOSUM62 features or learned embeddings capture residue similarity patterns relevant to immunogenicity. Models typically use binary classification with AUROC and AUPRC as key metrics. Reference: Vita et al., 'The Immune Epitope Database (IEDB)', Nucleic Acids Res 2019; Li et al., 'DeepImmuno: deep learning-empowered prediction and generation of immunogenic peptides', Brief Bioinform 2021.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np

class ImmunogenicityModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, aa_vocab=21, embed_dim=32, max_len=15, hla_dim=34):
        super().__init__()
        self.aa_embed = nn.Embedding(aa_vocab, embed_dim)
        self.peptide_encoder = nn.LSTM(embed_dim, 64, batch_first=True,
                                        bidirectional=True)
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(128 + hla_dim, 64), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.3), nn.Linear(64, 1))

    def forward(self, peptide_seq, hla_feat):
        emb = self.aa_embed(peptide_seq)
        output, (h_n, _) = self.peptide_encoder(emb)
        # Concatenate forward and backward final hidden states
        pep_repr = torch.cat([h_n[0], h_n[{{b1}}]], dim=-1)
        combined = torch.cat([pep_repr, hla_feat], dim=-1)
        return self.classifier(combined).squeeze(-1)

model = ImmunogenicityModel()
peptides = torch.randint(0, 21, (16, 9))  # 9-mer peptides
hla = torch.randn(16, 34)  # HLA pseudo-sequence features
labels = torch.randint(0, 2, (16,)).{{b2}}()

logits = model(peptides, hla)
loss = nn.{{b3}}()(logits, labels)
loss.backward()
print(f'Logits range: [{logits.min():.2f}, {logits.max():.2f}]')

blanks.hint: In a bidirectional LSTM, h_n[0] is forward and h_n[1] is backward final hidden state.
Convert integer labels to float for binary cross-entropy loss.
BCEWithLogitsLoss combines sigmoid and binary cross-entropy for numerical stability.

questions.title: MHC-I Binding Prediction with Peptide Encoding
questions.description: Build a model to predict MHC class I binding affinity for peptides using BLOSUM62-based amino acid encoding.
questions.info: MHC class I binding prediction is fundamental to neoantigen discovery and vaccine design. NetMHCpan (Jurtz et al., J Immunol 2017) is the gold standard, using artificial neural networks trained on binding affinity data from IEDB. Peptides are typically 8-11 amino acids long, and binding affinity is measured as IC50 in nM, often log-transformed. BLOSUM62 encoding represents each amino acid as a 20-dimensional vector capturing evolutionary substitution frequencies. The standard threshold for binding is IC50 < 500 nM (strong binder < 50 nM). Training data combines binding affinity (BA) and eluted ligand (EL) data for improved performance. Reference: Jurtz et al., 'NetMHCpan-4.0: Improved Peptide-MHC Class I Interaction Predictions', J Immunol 2017; O'Donnell et al., 'MHCflurry 2.0: Improved Pan-Allele Prediction of MHC Class I-Presented Peptides', Cell Systems 2020.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np

# Simplified BLOSUM62-like encoding (20 AA x 20 features)
blosum_matrix = np.random.randn(21, 20).astype(np.float32)  # placeholder

def encode_peptide(seq_indices, max_len=11):
    """Encode peptide using BLOSUM features, pad to max_len."""
    encoded = blosum_matrix[seq_indices]
    padded = np.zeros((max_len, 20), dtype=np.float32)
    padded[:len(seq_indices)] = encoded[:max_len]
    return padded

class MHCBindingPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, max_len=11, feat_dim=20, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.flatten_dim = max_len * feat_dim
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(self.flatten_dim, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.2),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, 1))

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (batch, max_len, feat_dim)
        x = x.{{b1}}(x.size(0), -1)  # flatten
        return self.net(x).squeeze(-1)

model = MHCBindingPredictor()
peptides = torch.randn(32, 11, 20)  # BLOSUM-encoded
ic50_log = torch.randn(32)  # log-transformed IC50

pred = model(peptides)
loss = nn.MSELoss()(pred, ic50_log)
loss.backward()
# Convert prediction to IC50 nM
ic50_pred = torch.{{b2}}(pred * -1)  # inverse log transform
binder_mask = ic50_pred {{b3}} 500  # binding threshold
print(f'Predicted binders: {binder_mask.sum().item()}')

blanks.hint: Use view() or reshape() to flatten the peptide encoding for the linear layer.
Use torch.exp() to convert log-transformed values back to the original IC50 scale.
A peptide is considered a binder if IC50 < 500 nM.

questions.title: TCR-Epitope Binding Prediction with Dual Encoders
questions.description: Implement a dual-encoder model predicting T-cell receptor (TCR) and epitope binding using CDR3 sequence embeddings.
questions.info: TCR-epitope interaction prediction aims to determine whether a T-cell receptor recognizes a specific peptide-MHC complex. The CDR3 region of the TCR beta chain is the primary determinant of epitope specificity. ERGO-II (Springer et al., Front Immunol 2021) and NetTCR (Montemurro et al., eLife 2022) use deep learning on CDR3 and epitope sequences for binding prediction. The VDJdb and McPAS-TCR databases provide paired TCR-epitope binding data. A major challenge is the extreme diversity of TCR sequences and limited positive training data. Negative sampling strategies (e.g., shuffled pairs) significantly impact model performance and must be carefully designed. Reference: Springer et al., 'Prediction of specific TCR-peptide binding from large dictionaries of TCR-peptide pairs', Front Immunol 2021; Montemurro et al., 'NetTCR-2.0 enables accurate prediction of TCR-peptide binding', eLife 2022.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class TCREpitopeDualEncoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab=21, embed=32, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.aa_embed = nn.Embedding(vocab, embed)
        self.tcr_encoder = nn.GRU(embed, hidden, batch_first=True)
        self.epi_encoder = nn.GRU(embed, hidden, batch_first=True)
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(hidden * 2, 64), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(64, 1))

    def forward(self, tcr_seq, epi_seq):
        tcr_emb = self.aa_embed(tcr_seq)
        epi_emb = self.aa_embed(epi_seq)
        _, tcr_h = self.tcr_encoder(tcr_emb)  # (1, batch, hidden)
        _, epi_h = self.epi_encoder(epi_emb)
        tcr_repr = tcr_h.{{b1}}(0)  # remove layer dim
        epi_repr = epi_h.squeeze(0)
        combined = torch.cat([tcr_repr, epi_repr], dim=-1)
        return self.classifier(combined).squeeze(-1)

model = TCREpitopeDualEncoder()
tcr = torch.randint(0, 21, (16, 15))  # CDR3 sequences, max len 15
epi = torch.randint(0, 21, (16, 9))   # epitope 9-mers
labels = torch.randint(0, 2, (16,)).float()

logits = model(tcr, epi)
loss = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()(logits, labels)
loss.backward()
prob = torch.{{b2}}(logits)
print(f'Binding probabilities: {prob[:5].detach().numpy().round({{b3}})}')

blanks.hint: Use squeeze(0) to remove the num_layers dimension from the GRU hidden state.
Apply sigmoid to convert raw logits to probabilities in [0, 1].
Round to 2-3 decimal places for readable output.

questions.title: Protein Expression Level Prediction from Codon Usage
questions.description: Train a regression model to predict recombinant protein expression levels in E. coli from codon usage statistics and mRNA features.
questions.info: Protein expression level prediction estimates how much recombinant protein can be produced in a given host organism. Codon usage bias is a primary determinant of expression, as rare codons can stall ribosomes and reduce yield. The Codon Adaptation Index (CAI) (Sharp & Li, Nucleic Acids Res 1987) measures codon optimality relative to highly expressed genes. Machine learning models use features like CAI, GC content, mRNA folding energy near the start codon, and rare codon clusters. The ECOD dataset of experimentally measured E. coli expression levels provides training data for supervised approaches. Random forests on codon usage features provide a robust baseline before attempting deep learning. Reference: Sharp & Li, 'The codon Adaptation Index--a measure of directional synonymous codon usage bias', Nucleic Acids Res 1987; Kudla et al., 'Coding-sequence determinants of gene expression in Escherichia coli', Science 2009.

questions.title: Codon Optimization Scoring with Sequence Models
questions.description: Implement a scoring function for codon-optimized sequences using a neural network that evaluates codon choices in the context of surrounding codons.
questions.info: Codon optimization replaces synonymous codons to maximize protein expression in a target host. Beyond single-codon frequency matching (CAI), context-dependent models consider codon pair bias and local mRNA structure effects. Codon pair bias (Coleman et al., Science 2008) measures the over- or under-representation of adjacent codon pairs relative to expectation. Neural network approaches can learn complex codon context preferences from highly expressed gene sequences. The model scores each codon position conditioned on its neighbors, similar to language modeling. Lattice et al. (Nature Biotech 2022) demonstrated that ML-optimized codon sequences significantly outperform rule-based optimization for mRNA therapeutics. Reference: Coleman et al., 'Virus attenuation by genome-scale changes in codon pair bias', Science 2008; Hanson & Coller, 'Codon optimality, bias and usage in translation and mRNA decay', Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol 2018.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class CodonContextScorer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_codons=64, embed_dim=32, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.codon_embed = nn.Embedding(n_codons, embed_dim)
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(embed_dim, hidden, batch_first=True,
                            bidirectional=True)
        # Predict score for each codon position
        self.scorer = nn.Linear(hidden * 2, 1)

    def forward(self, codon_seq):
        # codon_seq: (batch, n_codons_in_seq) codon indices
        emb = self.codon_embed(codon_seq)
        output, _ = self.lstm(emb)  # (batch, seq, hidden*2)
        scores = self.scorer(output).squeeze(-1)
        return scores

    def sequence_score(self, codon_seq):
        scores = self.forward(codon_seq)
        return scores.{{b1}}(dim=-1)  # total sequence score

model = CodonContextScorer()
# Gene of 100 codons, batch of 4 variants
original = torch.randint(0, 64, (4, 100))
optimized = torch.randint(0, 64, (4, 100))

orig_scores = model.sequence_score(original)
opt_scores = model.sequence_score(optimized)
improvement = opt_scores - orig_scores
print(f'Score improvement: {improvement.{{b2}}().detach().numpy():.4f}')

# Training: maximize scores for highly expressed genes
high_expr_codons = torch.randint(0, 64, (8, 100))
scores = model.sequence_score(high_expr_codons)
loss = -scores.{{b3}}()  # maximize by minimizing negative
loss.backward()
print(f'Training loss: {loss.item():.4f}')

questions.title: mRNA Stability Prediction from Sequence and Structure Features

questions.info: mRNA stability determines protein expression duration and is critical for mRNA therapeutics and gene regulation studies. The 5' UTR, 3' UTR, poly(A) tail length, and codon optimality all influence mRNA half-life. Agarwal & Kelley (Cell Reports 2022) used deep learning on mRNA sequences to predict stability with models trained on genome-wide mRNA decay measurements. Saluki (Agarwal & Kelley, Genome Biology 2023) further showed that transformer-based models capturing RNA secondary structure improve mRNA half-life prediction. Features like AU-rich elements in the 3' UTR, GC content, and minimum free energy of local RNA structure are informative. Training data comes from BRIC-seq or TimeLapse-seq experiments measuring transcriptome-wide mRNA decay rates. Reference: Agarwal & Kelley, 'The genetic and biochemical determinants of mRNA degradation rates in mammals', Genome Biology 2023; Wayment-Steele et al., 'RNA structure determination and design using DL', Nature Methods 2024.

import torch

class mRNAStabilityModel(nn.Module):

        )

    def forward(self, seq_features, struct_features):

questions.title: Intrinsically Disordered Region Prediction
questions.description: Implement a bidirectional LSTM model for per-residue prediction of intrinsic disorder from protein sequence features.
questions.info: Intrinsically disordered regions (IDRs) lack stable 3D structure and are involved in signaling, regulation, and phase separation. IUPred (Dosztanyi et al., Bioinformatics 2005) estimates disorder tendency from amino acid pairwise interaction energies. SPOT-Disorder (Hanson et al., Bioinformatics 2017) showed that deep learning on sequence profiles (PSSM) significantly improves disorder prediction over physicochemical approaches. ESMFold confidence scores (pLDDT) from AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) are now commonly used as disorder proxies, with low-confidence regions corresponding to disordered segments. The DisProt database provides curated experimental annotations of disordered regions for benchmarking. Bidirectional LSTMs capture long-range sequence context important for disorder boundary detection. Reference: Dosztanyi et al., 'IUPred: prediction of intrinsically unstructured proteins', Bioinformatics 2005; Jumper et al., 'Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold', Nature 2021.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class DisorderPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=20, hidden=128, n_layers=2):
        super().__init__()
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(input_dim, hidden, num_layers=n_layers,
                            batch_first=True, bidirectional={{b1}},
                            dropout=0.3)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden * 2, 1)  # *2 for bidirectional

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (batch, seq_len, input_dim)
        output, _ = self.lstm(x)  # (batch, seq_len, hidden*2)
        disorder_scores = torch.sigmoid(self.fc(output)).squeeze(-1)
        return disorder_scores

model = DisorderPredictor()
seq_features = torch.randn(8, 200, 20)  # PSSM features
labels = torch.randint(0, 2, (8, 200)).float()  # 1=disordered

pred = model(seq_features)
loss = nn.{{b2}}()(pred, labels)
loss.backward()

# Evaluate: threshold at 0.5
pred_binary = (pred > {{b3}}).float()
acc = (pred_binary == labels).float().mean()
print(f'Disorder prediction accuracy: {acc.item():.4f}')

blanks.hint: Set bidirectional=True to read the sequence in both forward and reverse directions.
Use BCELoss since sigmoid is already applied in the model (or use BCEWithLogitsLoss without sigmoid).
A threshold of 0.5 is the standard decision boundary for binary classification.

questions.title: Liquid-Liquid Phase Separation Propensity Prediction
questions.description: Build a model to predict protein propensity for liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) using sequence composition, disorder features, and charge patterning.
questions.info: Liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) drives the formation of membraneless organelles like stress granules and nucleoli. Proteins that undergo LLPS are enriched in intrinsically disordered regions, low-complexity sequences, and multivalent interaction domains. The PhaSePro database (Meszaros et al., Nucleic Acids Res 2020) catalogs experimentally validated phase-separating proteins for ML training. Key predictive features include charge patterning (kappa parameter), aromatic residue content, prion-like domain composition, and pi-pi stacking capacity. DeePhase (Saar et al., PNAS 2021) used gradient boosting on sequence features and achieved high accuracy distinguishing LLPS-prone from non-LLPS proteins. The sequence valence framework captures how multivalent interactions drive phase separation. Reference: Meszaros et al., 'PhaSePro: the database of proteins driving LLPS', Nucleic Acids Res 2020; Saar et al., 'Learning the molecular grammar of protein condensates', PNAS 2021.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np

class LLPSPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, comp_dim=20, pattern_dim=15, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(comp_dim + pattern_dim, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(), nn.BatchNorm1d(hidden),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(0.3),
            nn.Linear(hidden, 1))

    def forward(self, composition, charge_pattern):
        x = torch.{{b1}}([composition, charge_pattern], dim=-1)
        return self.net(x).squeeze(-1)

def compute_kappa(charges, seq_len, blob_size=5):
    """Compute charge patterning parameter kappa."""
    n_blobs = seq_len // blob_size
    sigma_max = np.sqrt(np.mean(charges**2))
    if sigma_max == 0:
        return 0.0
    blob_charges = [charges[i*blob_size:(i+1)*blob_size].mean()
                    for i in range(n_blobs)]
    sigma_seq = np.std(blob_charges)
    return (sigma_seq / sigma_max) if sigma_max > 0 else 0.0

model = LLPSPredictor()
comp = torch.randn(16, 20)   # AA composition
pattern = torch.randn(16, 15) # charge patterning features
labels = torch.randint(0, 2, (16,)).float()

logits = model(comp, pattern)
loss = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()(logits, labels)
loss.backward()
prob = torch.sigmoid(logits)
llps_pred = (prob > 0.5).{{b2}}()
print(f'Predicted LLPS-prone: {llps_pred.sum().{{b3}}()}/{len(labels)}')

blanks.hint: Use torch.cat to concatenate composition and charge pattern features.
Convert boolean predictions to integer type for counting.
Use .item() to extract the sum as a Python integer.

questions.title: Allosteric Binding Site Detection from Structural Features
questions.description: Implement a residue-level classifier to identify allosteric binding sites using features derived from protein structure and dynamics.
questions.info: Allosteric sites are regulatory binding pockets distant from the active site that modulate protein function upon ligand binding. Detecting allosteric sites is valuable for drug design as allosteric drugs can achieve higher selectivity. The ASBench dataset (Huang et al., Bioinformatics 2015) provides known allosteric sites for benchmarking prediction methods. PASSer (Tian et al., J Chem Inf Model 2023) uses graph neural networks on residue contact graphs with structural features for allosteric site prediction. Key features include residue depth, pocket volume, local flexibility (B-factors), evolutionary conservation, and normal mode analysis perturbation scores. Allosteric communication pathways can be detected through dynamical network analysis of molecular dynamics simulations. Reference: Huang et al., 'ASBench: benchmarking sets for allosteric discovery', Bioinformatics 2015; Tian et al., 'PASSer: fast and accurate prediction of protein allosteric sites', J Chem Inf Model 2023.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class AllostericSiteDetector(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, node_feat=32, edge_feat=8, hidden=64, n_layers=3):
        super().__init__()
        self.node_embed = nn.Linear(node_feat, hidden)
        self.edge_embed = nn.Linear(edge_feat, hidden)
        self.convs = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.Linear(hidden * 2, hidden) for _ in range(n_layers)])
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(hidden, 2)  # allosteric vs non

    def forward(self, node_feats, edge_feats, adj):
        h = self.node_embed(node_feats)
        e = self.edge_embed(edge_feats.mean(dim=1))  # simplified
        for conv in self.convs:
            neighbor_msg = adj @ h
            combined = torch.cat([h, neighbor_msg], dim={{b1}})
            h = torch.relu(conv(combined))
        return self.classifier(h)

n_residues = 150
node_feat = torch.randn(n_residues, 32)  # depth, bfactor, conservation...
edge_feat = torch.randn(n_residues, n_residues, 8)
adj = (torch.randn(n_residues, n_residues) > 1.0).float()  # contact map
adj = (adj + adj.T).clamp(max=1)  # symmetric
adj = adj / adj.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True).clamp(min=1)

model = AllostericSiteDetector()
logits = model(node_feat, edge_feat, adj)
labels = torch.zeros(n_residues, dtype=torch.long)
labels[50:60] = 1  # allosteric site residues

loss = nn.{{b2}}()(logits, labels)
loss.backward()
pred_sites = logits.{{b3}}(dim=-1)
print(f'Predicted allosteric residues: {(pred_sites == 1).sum().item()}')

blanks.hint: Concatenate self and neighbor features along the last (feature) dimension.
CrossEntropyLoss handles multi-class (allosteric vs non-allosteric) classification.
Use argmax along the class dimension to get the predicted label for each residue.

questions.title: Cryptic Binding Site Detection from Conformational Ensembles
questions.description: Build a model to detect cryptic (hidden) binding sites by comparing structural features across conformational ensembles.
questions.info: Cryptic binding sites are pockets that are not visible in a single static structure but become exposed through conformational dynamics. CryptoSite (Cimermancic et al., J Mol Biol 2016) pioneered ML-based cryptic site prediction by training on pairs of apo/holo structures. Features include local flexibility, pocket propensity changes between conformations, and evolutionary conservation. AlphaFold2 predicted structures with varying confidence can sample conformational diversity relevant to cryptic pocket opening. PocketMiner (Meller et al., Nature Communications 2023) uses graph neural networks on MD simulation snapshots to identify transient pockets. The key insight is that residues near cryptic sites show higher local structural variability across the ensemble. Reference: Cimermancic et al., 'CryptoSite: expanding the druggable proteome by characterization of cryptic binding sites', J Mol Biol 2016; Meller et al., 'Predicting locations of cryptic pockets from single protein structures using the PocketMiner GNN', Nature Communications 2023.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np

class CrypticSiteDetector(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, feat_dim=40, hidden=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(feat_dim, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU())
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)

    def forward(self, ensemble_features):
        # ensemble_features: (n_residues, feat_dim)
        h = self.encoder(ensemble_features)
        return self.classifier(h).squeeze(-1)

def compute_ensemble_features(conformations):
    """Compute per-residue features from structural ensemble.
    conformations: (n_conf, n_residues, 3) CA coordinates"""
    mean_pos = conformations.mean(axis=0)
    rmsf = np.sqrt(((conformations - mean_pos)**2).{{b1}}(axis=0).mean(axis=-1))
    # Compute SASA variance, pocket scores etc. (simplified)
    variance_features = conformations.{{b2}}(axis=0).mean(axis=-1)
    return np.column_stack([rmsf, variance_features,
                           np.random.randn(len(rmsf), 38)])

n_residues = 200
n_conf = 50
conformations = np.random.randn(n_conf, n_residues, 3)
features = compute_ensemble_features(conformations)

model = CrypticSiteDetector()
feat_tensor = torch.tensor(features, dtype=torch.float32)
scores = model(feat_tensor)
labels = torch.zeros(n_residues)
labels[80:90] = 1.0  # known cryptic site

loss = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss(pos_weight=torch.tensor([{{b3}}]))(scores, labels)
loss.backward()
print(f'Loss: {loss.item():.4f}')

blanks.hint: Average the squared deviations across all conformations for RMSF calculation.
Compute standard deviation or variance across conformations to measure structural variability.
Use a high positive weight (e.g., 10-20) to handle the imbalance between cryptic site and non-site residues.

questions.title: Protein Engineering Fitness Scoring with Ensemble Models
questions.description: Implement an ensemble scoring function combining evolutionary, structural, and learned features for ranking protein engineering variants.
questions.info: Protein engineering scoring functions rank designed variants by predicted fitness to guide experimental testing. Ensemble methods combining diverse feature sources consistently outperform single-model approaches. EVmutation (Hopf et al., Nature Biotech 2017) provides evolutionary coupling-based scores from multiple sequence alignments. ESM-1v (Meier et al., NeurIPS 2021) computes zero-shot mutation scores using protein language model log-likelihoods. Rosetta energy functions provide physics-based stability estimates (ddG). The ProteinGym benchmark (Notin et al., NeurIPS 2023) evaluates predictor combinations on hundreds of DMS assays, showing that ensemble approaches combining evolutionary, language model, and structural predictors achieve the highest average Spearman correlations. Reference: Notin et al., 'ProteinGym: Large-Scale Benchmarks for Protein Fitness Prediction and Design', NeurIPS 2023; Meier et al., 'Language models enable zero-shot prediction of the effects of mutations on protein function', NeurIPS 2021.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from scipy.stats import spearmanr, rankdata

np.random.seed(42)
n_variants = 300

# Individual predictor scores
ev_scores = np.random.randn(n_variants)      # EVmutation
esm_scores = np.random.randn(n_variants)     # ESM-1v
rosetta_scores = np.random.randn(n_variants)  # Rosetta ddG
y_true = np.random.randn(n_variants)          # experimental fitness

# Stack features for ensemble
X = np.column_stack([ev_scores, esm_scores, rosetta_scores])

# Rank-based normalization
X_ranked = np.apply_along_axis({{b1}}, 0, X) / n_variants

# Fit ensemble on first 200, evaluate on last 100
X_tr, X_te = X_ranked[:200], X_ranked[200:]
y_tr, y_te = y_true[:200], y_true[200:]

ensemble = Ridge(alpha=1.0)
ensemble.fit(X_tr, y_tr)
y_pred = ensemble.predict(X_te)
rho, _ = spearmanr(y_te, y_pred)

# Compare individual vs ensemble
for name, scores in [('EVmutation', ev_scores[200:]),
                     ('ESM-1v', esm_scores[200:]),
                     ('Rosetta', rosetta_scores[200:])]:
    r, _ = spearmanr(y_te, scores)
    print(f'{name}: rho={r:.4f}')
print(f'Ensemble: rho={rho:.4f}')
print(f'Weights: {dict({{b2}}([\'Ev\',\'ESM\',\'Rosetta\'], ensemble.{{b3}}))}')

blanks.hint: Use rankdata to convert scores to ranks for normalization.
Use zip() to pair feature names with their learned ensemble weights.
Access coef_ attribute to get the learned Ridge regression weights.

questions.title: ML-Guided Directed Evolution with Bayesian Optimization
questions.description: Implement a Bayesian optimization loop for ML-guided directed evolution that selects the most informative variants to test experimentally.
questions.info: ML-guided directed evolution (MLDE) uses machine learning to propose high-fitness variants between experimental rounds, dramatically reducing screening costs. Bayesian optimization with Gaussian Process (GP) surrogates balances exploration of uncertain regions with exploitation of predicted high-fitness variants. Wu et al. (PNAS 2019) demonstrated that MLDE can find optimal variants while testing fewer than 1% of a combinatorial library. The acquisition function (e.g., Expected Improvement, Upper Confidence Bound) determines which variants to test next. Thompson sampling provides another effective acquisition strategy by sampling from the posterior predictive distribution. Each round consists of: fit GP on observed data, compute acquisition scores, select batch, observe experimentally, repeat. Reference: Wu et al., 'Machine learning-assisted directed protein evolution with combinatorial libraries', PNAS 2019; Yang et al., 'Machine-learning-guided directed evolution for protein engineering', Nature Methods 2019.
questions.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.gaussian_process import GaussianProcessRegressor
from sklearn.gaussian_process.kernels import RBF, ConstantKernel

np.random.seed(42)
n_total = 1000
X_all = np.random.randn(n_total, 10)  # variant encodings
y_all = np.sin(X_all[:, 0]) + 0.5 * X_all[:, 1]  # true fitness

# Start with 20 random variants
initial_n = 20
observed_idx = list(np.random.choice(n_total, initial_n, replace=False))

def upper_confidence_bound(mu, sigma, beta=2.0):
    """UCB acquisition: balance exploration and exploitation."""
    return mu + beta * {{b1}}

for round_num in range(5):
    X_obs = X_all[observed_idx]
    y_obs = y_all[observed_idx]
    
    kernel = ConstantKernel() * RBF()
    gp = GaussianProcessRegressor(kernel=kernel, random_state=42)
    gp.fit(X_obs, y_obs)
    
    # Score all unobserved variants
    unobserved = [i for i in range(n_total) if i not in observed_idx]
    X_cand = X_all[unobserved]
    mu, sigma = gp.predict(X_cand, return_std={{b2}})
    
    acq_scores = upper_confidence_bound(mu, sigma)
    # Select top-5 for next round
    top_k = np.argsort(acq_scores)[{{b3}}:]
    new_idx = [unobserved[i] for i in top_k]
    observed_idx.extend(new_idx)
    
    best = y_all[observed_idx].max()
    print(f'Round {round_num+1}: tested={len(observed_idx)}, best={best:.3f}')

blanks.hint: UCB adds beta times the standard deviation (sigma) to the mean prediction to encourage exploring uncertain regions.
Set return_std=True to get both mean predictions and standard deviations from the GP.
Slice the last 5 elements from the sorted indices to get the 5 highest acquisition scores.

questions.title: B-Cell Epitope Prediction from Sequence and Structure
questions.description: Build a residue-level classifier to predict linear B-cell epitopes using sequence features like hydrophilicity, accessibility, and flexibility.
questions.info: B-cell epitope prediction identifies protein surface regions recognized by antibodies, crucial for vaccine design and diagnostic development. Linear B-cell epitopes are continuous sequence stretches of 5-20 residues. BepiPred-2.0 (Jespersen et al., Nucleic Acids Res 2017) uses random forests on sequence and structural features, and BepiPred-3.0 uses ESM-2 embeddings with improved accuracy. Key predictive features include solvent accessibility, hydrophilicity (Parker scale), flexibility (Karplus-Schulz), and turns propensity. Epitope datasets are inherently imbalanced since most residues are non-epitope, requiring careful handling of class imbalance. The IEDB provides curated experimental epitope mapping data from antibody-antigen co-crystal structures. Reference: Jespersen et al., 'BepiPred-2.0: improving sequence-based B-cell epitope prediction using conformational epitopes', Nucleic Acids Res 2017; Clifford et al., 'BepiPred-3.0: Improved B-cell epitope prediction using protein language models', Protein Science 2022.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class BcellEpitopePredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, feat_dim=30, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv1d(feat_dim, hidden, kernel_size=9, padding=4)
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv1d(hidden, hidden, kernel_size=5, padding=2)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(0.2)

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (batch, seq_len, feat_dim)
        h = x.transpose(1, 2)  # (batch, feat_dim, seq_len)
        h = torch.relu(self.conv1(h))
        h = self.dropout(h)
        h = torch.relu(self.conv2(h))
        h = h.transpose(1, 2)  # back to (batch, seq_len, hidden)
        return self.fc(h).{{b1}}(-1)

model = BcellEpitopePredictor()
seq_feats = torch.randn(4, 200, 30)  # hydrophilicity, accessibility, etc.
labels = torch.zeros(4, 200)
labels[:, 50:65] = 1.0  # epitope region

# Handle class imbalance with pos_weight
n_pos = labels.sum()
n_neg = labels.numel() - n_pos
pos_weight = torch.tensor([n_neg / n_pos])
loss = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss(pos_weight={{b2}})

logits = model(seq_feats)
total_loss = loss(logits, labels)
total_loss.backward()
pred = (torch.{{b3}}(logits) > 0.5).float()
print(f'Predicted epitope residues: {pred.sum().item():.0f}')

blanks.hint: Use squeeze(-1) to remove the trailing dimension of size 1 from the linear output.
Pass the computed pos_weight tensor to weight the positive class higher.
Apply sigmoid to convert logits to probabilities for thresholding.

questions.title: Conformational B-Cell Epitope Prediction with GNN
questions.description: Implement a graph neural network for predicting conformational (discontinuous) B-cell epitopes using the protein residue contact graph.
questions.info: Conformational epitopes consist of residues that are distant in sequence but proximal in 3D structure, constituting over 90% of B-cell epitopes. Graph neural networks naturally model spatial residue proximity through contact-based edges. DiscoTope (Kringelum et al., PLoS Comput Biol 2012) was among the first structure-based epitope predictors, using spatial neighborhood statistics. EPMP (Dai & Bailey-Kellogg, Bioinformatics 2021) uses message passing on residue contact graphs with evolutionary and geometric features. Node features typically include solvent accessibility, B-factors, conservation scores, and amino acid properties. The protein graph is constructed with edges between residues within a distance cutoff (e.g., 8-10 Angstroms between C-alpha atoms). Reference: Kringelum et al., 'Reliable B cell epitope predictions: impacts of method development and improved benchmarking', PLoS Comput Biol 2012; Dai & Bailey-Kellogg, 'Protein interaction interface region prediction by geometric deep learning', Bioinformatics 2021.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class EpitopeGNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, node_dim=40, hidden=64, n_layers=3):
        super().__init__()
        self.input_proj = nn.Linear(node_dim, hidden)
        self.gnn_layers = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden) for _ in range(n_layers)])
        self.norms = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.LayerNorm(hidden) for _ in range(n_layers)])
        self.output = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)

    def forward(self, node_feats, adj_norm):
        h = torch.relu(self.input_proj(node_feats))
        for conv, norm in zip(self.gnn_layers, self.norms):
            msg = {{b1}} @ h  # neighbor aggregation
            h = torch.relu(norm(conv(msg))) + h  # residual
        return self.output(h).squeeze(-1)

# Build contact graph from C-alpha coordinates
n_res = 180
coords = torch.randn(n_res, 3) * 10  # C-alpha coordinates
dist_matrix = torch.cdist(coords.unsqueeze(0), coords.unsqueeze(0)).squeeze(0)
contact_map = (dist_matrix < {{b2}}).float()  # 8A cutoff
contact_map.fill_diagonal_(1.0)  # self-loops
adj_norm = contact_map / contact_map.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True)

node_feats = torch.randn(n_res, 40)  # ASA, B-factor, conservation, ...
model = EpitopeGNN()
scores = model(node_feats, adj_norm)

labels = torch.zeros(n_res)
labels[torch.randint(0, n_res, (20,))] = 1.0  # epitope residues
loss = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()(scores, labels)
loss.{{b3}}()
print(f'Epitope score range: [{scores.min():.2f}, {scores.max():.2f}]')

blanks.hint: Multiply the normalized adjacency matrix by node features for message passing.
Use 8 or 10 Angstroms as the C-alpha contact distance cutoff.
Call backward() to compute gradients through the computational graph.

questions.title: Aqueous Drug Solubility Prediction with Molecular Fingerprints

questions.info: Aqueous solubility is one of the most important physicochemical properties in drug development, as poorly soluble compounds have low oral bioavailability. Solubility is typically expressed as logS (log of molar solubility). The AqSolDB dataset (Sorkun et al., Scientific Data 2019) provides over 9,000 experimental solubility measurements for benchmarking. Extended-connectivity fingerprints (ECFP/Morgan fingerprints) encode molecular substructures as fixed-length binary or count vectors. The Delaney solubility dataset is a smaller but widely used benchmark with 1,144 compounds. Simple models like random forests on ECFP4 fingerprints achieve RMSE around 0.7-1.0 logS units, competitive with more complex approaches. Reference: Sorkun et al., 'AqSolDB, a curated reference set of aqueous solubility', Scientific Data 2019; Delaney, 'ESOL: Estimating Aqueous Solubility Directly from Molecular Structure', J Chem Inf Comput Sci 2004.

questions.title: Protein-Ligand Interaction Fingerprint Classification
questions.description: Build a classifier using protein-ligand interaction fingerprints (PLIF) to distinguish active binders from decoys based on interaction patterns.
questions.info: Protein-ligand interaction fingerprints (PLIFs) encode the types of interactions (hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic contacts, pi-stacking, salt bridges) between a ligand and binding site residues. PLIFs provide interpretable descriptors that capture binding mode information lost in simple molecular fingerprints. The ProLIF library (Bouysset & Fiorucci, J Cheminformatics 2021) computes PLIFs from docking poses or MD trajectories. PLIF-based models can distinguish true binders from decoys that happen to dock well but lack key pharmacophoric interactions. Each bit in the fingerprint represents a specific interaction type at a specific binding site residue. Combining PLIFs with molecular descriptors often improves virtual screening performance over either representation alone. Reference: Bouysset & Fiorucci, 'ProLIF: a library to encode molecular interactions as fingerprints', J Cheminformatics 2021; Da & Bhatt, 'Harnessing the Power of Interaction Fingerprints', J Chem Inf Model 2014.
