The positive class represents active (binding) compounds.

questions.title: MHC Class II Binding Prediction for CD4+ T-Cell Epitopes
questions.description: Implement a convolutional model for predicting MHC class II binding peptides, handling variable peptide lengths and the open binding groove.
questions.info: MHC class II molecules present longer peptides (13-25 residues) to CD4+ helper T cells, with an open binding groove allowing peptide ends to extend beyond the molecule. The 9-residue binding core within the longer peptide is the critical determinant of binding specificity. NetMHCIIpan (Reynisson et al., Nucleic Acids Res 2020) identifies the binding core position as part of the prediction. Training data includes both binding affinity measurements and eluted ligand mass spectrometry data from the IEDB. MHC-II alleles are represented by pseudo-sequences capturing the polymorphic residues lining the binding groove. The binding core identification problem makes MHC-II prediction harder than MHC-I, as the model must learn which 9-mer within a longer peptide is presented. Reference: Reynisson et al., 'NetMHCpan-4.1 and NetMHCIIpan-4.0: improved predictions of MHC antigen presentation', Nucleic Acids Res 2020; Abelin et al., 'Defining HLA-II Ligand Processing and Binding Rules with Mass Spectrometry', Immunity 2019.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class MHCIIPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, aa_vocab=21, embed_dim=32, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(aa_vocab, embed_dim)
        # Multiple kernel sizes to capture binding cores
        self.conv9 = nn.Conv1d(embed_dim, hidden, kernel_size=9)
        self.conv11 = nn.Conv1d(embed_dim, hidden, kernel_size=11)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden * 2, 1)

    def forward(self, peptide_seq):
        # peptide_seq: (batch, max_len) padded token indices
        emb = self.embed(peptide_seq).transpose(1, 2)
        # Global max pool captures strongest binding core signal
        c9 = self.conv9(emb)
        c11 = self.conv11(emb)
        p9 = c9.max(dim={{b1}})[0]   # (batch, hidden)
        p11 = c11.max(dim=-1)[0]
        combined = torch.cat([p9, p11], dim=-1)
        return self.fc(combined).squeeze(-1)

model = MHCIIPredictor()
peptides = torch.randint(0, 21, (32, 25))  # max length 25
affinity = torch.randn(32)  # log-transformed IC50

pred = model(peptides)
loss = nn.{{b2}}()(pred, affinity)

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
optimizer.{{b3}}()
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()
print(f'Prediction range: [{pred.min():.2f}, {pred.max():.2f}]')

blanks.hint: Max-pool along the sequence (last) dimension to find the strongest activation for any binding core position.
Use MSELoss or another regression loss for binding affinity prediction.
Call zero_grad() before backward() to clear accumulated gradients from the previous step.

questions.title: Thermal Stability (Tm) Prediction for Protein Engineering
questions.description: Train a model to predict protein melting temperature (Tm) from global sequence and structural features for thermostability engineering.
questions.info: Thermal stability (melting temperature, Tm) is a key target for protein engineering in industrial and therapeutic applications. ProThermDB (Nikam et al., Nucleic Acids Res 2021) provides experimentally measured thermodynamic stability data for thousands of protein variants. Sequence-derived features like amino acid composition (especially proline and charged residue content), hydrophobic core packing, and salt bridge counts correlate with Tm. Thermophilic organism proteins provide positive examples with naturally high Tm values for comparative studies. FireProtDB (Stourac et al., Nucleic Acids Res 2021) aggregates stabilizing mutation data useful for engineering. Simple regression models on curated features serve as interpretable baselines before applying deep learning. Reference: Nikam et al., 'ProThermDB: thermodynamic database for proteins and mutants revisited', Nucleic Acids Res 2021; Stourac et al., 'FireProtDB: database of manually curated protein stability data', Nucleic Acids Res 2021.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import Lasso
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

np.random.seed(42)
n = 400
# Features: AA composition (20), length, MW, pI, gravy,
# helix%, sheet%, disorder%
X = np.random.randn(n, 27)
y = np.random.normal(55.0, 15.0, n)  # Tm in Celsius

model = Lasso(alpha={{b1}})

# 5-fold CV with negative MAE scoring
scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5,
                          scoring='neg_mean_absolute_error')
mae_scores = -scores  # convert negative to positive
print(f'CV MAE: {mae_scores.{{b2}}():.2f} +/- {mae_scores.std():.2f} deg C')

# Final model
model.fit(X, y)
coef_nonzero = np.sum(model.coef_ != {{b3}})
print(f'Non-zero coefficients: {coef_nonzero}/{X.shape[1]}')
print(f'Feature selection via L1 regularization')

Compute the mean of MAE scores across folds.
Lasso (L1) regularization drives some coefficients exactly to zero.

questions.title: Signal Peptide and Subcellular Localization Prediction
questions.description: Build a model that predicts signal peptide presence and cleavage site position from the N-terminal region of protein sequences.
questions.info: Signal peptides are short N-terminal sequences (15-30 residues) directing proteins to the secretory pathway, with a conserved tripartite structure: positive n-region, hydrophobic h-region, and cleavage c-region. SignalP 6.0 (Teufel et al., Nature Biotech 2022) uses protein language models to predict five types of signal peptides across all domains of life. The cleavage site follows the (-3, -1) rule where small neutral residues (Ala, Gly, Ser) occupy positions -1 and -3. Training data comes from UniProt signal peptide annotations and experimentally validated N-terminal sequences. Accurate signal peptide prediction is essential for recombinant protein production and secretome analysis. The model must distinguish signal peptides from transmembrane helices, which share hydrophobic character. Reference: Teufel et al., 'SignalP 6.0 predicts all five types of signal peptides using protein language models', Nature Biotech 2022; Almagro Armenteros et al., 'SignalP 5.0 improves signal peptide predictions', Nature Biotech 2019.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SignalPeptidePredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab=21, embed=32, hidden=64, max_len=70):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(vocab, embed)
        self.encoder = nn.LSTM(embed, hidden, batch_first=True,
                               bidirectional=True)
        # Per-position classification: SP-region labels
        self.position_head = nn.Linear(hidden * 2, 4)  # n/h/c/non-SP
        # Global: has signal peptide?
        self.global_head = nn.Linear(hidden * 2, 1)

    def forward(self, seq):
        emb = self.embed(seq)
        output, (h_n, _) = self.encoder(emb)
        pos_logits = self.position_head(output)  # per-residue
        # Global prediction from mean pooling
        global_repr = output.{{b1}}(dim=1)
        global_logit = self.global_head(global_repr).squeeze(-1)
        return pos_logits, global_logit

model = SignalPeptidePredictor()
seqs = torch.randint(0, 21, (8, 70))  # N-terminal 70 residues
region_labels = torch.randint(0, 4, (8, 70))  # n/h/c/non-SP
has_sp = torch.randint(0, 2, (8,)).float()

pos_logits, glob_logit = model(seqs)
pos_loss = nn.{{b2}}()(pos_logits.view(-1, 4), region_labels.view(-1))
glob_loss = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()(glob_logit, has_sp)
total_loss = pos_loss + {{b3}} * glob_loss
total_loss.backward()
print(f'Position loss: {pos_loss.item():.4f}, Global loss: {glob_loss.item():.4f}')

blanks.hint: Mean pool over the sequence dimension to get a fixed-size representation for global classification.
Use CrossEntropyLoss for the 4-class per-residue region classification (n/h/c/non-SP).
Weight the global loss relative to the position loss (e.g., 0.5 or 1.0).

questions.title: Binding Affinity Change Prediction for Protein-Protein Interface Mutations
questions.description: Build a model to predict changes in binding free energy (ddG) upon mutation at protein-protein interfaces using structural and evolutionary features.
questions.info: Predicting binding affinity changes (ddG_bind) upon mutation is essential for understanding disease mutations and engineering protein complexes like antibodies. The SKEMPI 2.0 database (Jankauskaite et al., Bioinformatics 2019) provides 7,085 experimental ddG values for interface mutations. Key features include change in interface buried surface area, electrostatic complementarity shifts, and evolutionary conservation at the mutation site. mCSM-PPI2 (Rodrigues et al., Nucleic Acids Res 2019) uses graph-based signatures to capture the structural environment around mutations. Antisymmetry is an important property: ddG(A->B) should approximately equal -ddG(B->A), which can be enforced as an architectural constraint. Models are typically evaluated using Pearson correlation and classification accuracy for stabilizing vs. destabilizing mutations. Reference: Jankauskaite et al., 'SKEMPI 2.0: an updated benchmark of changes in protein-protein binding energy', Bioinformatics 2019; Rodrigues et al., 'mCSM-PPI2: predicting the effects of mutations on protein-protein interactions', Nucleic Acids Res 2019.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np

class BindingDDGPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, feat_dim=50, hidden=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(feat_dim, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(0.2),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, 1))

    def forward(self, wt_features, mut_features):
        # Antisymmetric: swap WT/mut should flip sign
        delta = mut_features - wt_features
        return self.encoder(delta).squeeze(-1)

model = BindingDDGPredictor()
wt_feat = torch.randn(32, 50)   # structural env of WT residue
mut_feat = torch.randn(32, 50)  # structural env of mutant
ddg_true = torch.randn(32)       # experimental ddG

pred = model(wt_feat, mut_feat)
loss = nn.SmoothL1Loss()(pred, ddg_true)
loss.backward()

# Verify antisymmetry
pred_forward = model(wt_feat, mut_feat)
pred_reverse = model(mut_feat, wt_feat)
asymmetry = (pred_forward + pred_reverse).{{b1}}().item()
print(f'Asymmetry check (should be ~0): {asymmetry:.4f}')

# Classification: stabilizing vs destabilizing
pred_class = (pred > {{b2}}).long()  # positive ddG = destabilizing
true_class = (ddg_true > 0).long()
acc = (pred_class == true_class).float().{{b3}}()
print(f'Stab/Destab accuracy: {acc.item():.4f}')

blanks.hint: Take the mean of the sum to check how close forward + reverse predictions are to zero (antisymmetry).
Positive ddG values indicate destabilizing mutations; threshold at 0.
Take the mean of the boolean comparison to get classification accuracy.

questions.title: Evolutionary Conservation Scoring for Functional Site Identification
questions.description: Compute position-specific conservation scores from a multiple sequence alignment (MSA) and use them to identify functionally important residues.
questions.info: Evolutionary conservation is one of the strongest signals for identifying functional sites, as critical residues are under purifying selection. Shannon entropy measures conservation at each MSA column, with low entropy indicating high conservation. The ConSurf method (Ashkenazy et al., Nucleic Acids Res 2016) maps conservation scores onto protein structures to identify functional regions. Rate4Site (Pupko et al., Mol Biol Evol 2002) uses phylogenetic rate estimation for more accurate conservation scoring. Active sites, binding interfaces, and structural core residues all show elevated conservation. Conservation scores complement machine learning predictions by providing an orthogonal evolutionary signal. Reference: Ashkenazy et al., 'ConSurf 2016: an improved methodology to estimate and visualize evolutionary conservation', Nucleic Acids Res 2016; Pupko et al., 'Rate4Site: an algorithmic tool for the identification of functional regions', Mol Biol Evol 2002.
questions.code:
import numpy as np

def shannon_entropy(column):
    """Compute Shannon entropy of an MSA column."""
    aa_counts = {}
    total = 0
    for aa in column:
        if aa != '-':  # skip gaps
            aa_counts[aa] = aa_counts.get(aa, 0) + 1
            total += 1
    if total == 0:
        return 0.0
    entropy = 0.0
    for count in aa_counts.values():
        p = count / total
        entropy -= p * np.{{b1}}(p)
    return entropy

np.random.seed(42)
n_seqs, seq_len = 100, 200
amino_acids = list('ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVWY')

# Simulate MSA: mostly conserved with some variable positions
msa = np.array([[amino_acids[np.random.randint(0, 20)]
                  for _ in range(seq_len)] for _ in range(n_seqs)])
# Make some positions highly conserved (functional sites)
for pos in [45, 46, 100, 101, 102]:  # active site
    msa[:, pos] = 'H'  # all histidine
    msa[np.random.choice(n_seqs, 5), pos] = amino_acids[
        np.random.randint(0, 20)]  # few mutations

conservation = np.array([shannon_entropy(msa[:, i])
                          for i in range(seq_len)])
# Low entropy = high conservation
threshold = np.{{b2}}(conservation, 10)  # 10th percentile
conserved_positions = np.where(conservation <= threshold)[0]

print(f'Most conserved positions: {conserved_positions[:{{b3}}]}')
print(f'Mean entropy conserved: {conservation[conserved_positions].mean():.3f}')
print(f'Mean entropy overall: {conservation.mean():.3f}')

blanks.hint: Use log2 (or natural log) to compute Shannon entropy from amino acid frequencies.
Use np.percentile to find the 10th percentile of entropy values as the conservation threshold.
Display the first 10-20 most conserved positions.

questions.title: Protein-Protein Docking Pose Scoring with Deep Learning
questions.description: Implement a neural network scoring function to rank protein-protein docking poses using interface residue pair features.
questions.info: Protein-protein docking generates candidate complex structures that must be scored to identify near-native poses. Traditional scoring functions (ZRANK, pyDock) use physics-based energy terms, while deep learning approaches learn scoring functions from structural data. The ZDOCK/ZRANK benchmark (Pierce & Weng, Proteins 2007) provides docking decoy sets for evaluating scoring methods. Interface features include residue-residue distance distributions, contact counts, shape complementarity, and electrostatic complementarity. DL-based scoring functions like DeepRank (Renaud et al., Nature Communications 2021) use 3D CNNs or GNNs on interface residue graphs. The CAPRI assessment (Lensink et al., Proteins 2020) provides community-wide evaluation of docking and scoring methods. Reference: Renaud et al., 'DeepRank: a deep learning framework for data mining 3D protein-protein interfaces', Nature Communications 2021; Lensink et al., 'Prediction of protein assemblies, the next frontier: The CAPRI experiment', Proteins 2020.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class DockingScoringFunction(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, pair_feat_dim=40, hidden=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.pair_encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(pair_feat_dim, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU())
        self.attention = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)
        self.scorer = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)

    def forward(self, pair_features, mask):
        # pair_features: (batch, max_pairs, pair_feat_dim)
        # mask: (batch, max_pairs) 1=real pair, 0=padding
        h = self.pair_encoder(pair_features)
        # Attention-weighted aggregation of interface pairs
        attn_logits = self.attention(h).squeeze(-1)
        attn_logits = attn_logits.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))
        attn_weights = torch.softmax(attn_logits, dim={{b1}})
        interface_repr = (h * attn_weights.unsqueeze(-1)).sum(dim=1)
        return self.scorer(interface_repr).squeeze(-1)

model = DockingScoringFunction()
n_poses = 100  # docking decoys
pair_feats = torch.randn(n_poses, 50, 40)  # max 50 interface pairs
mask = torch.ones(n_poses, 50)
mask[:, 30:] = 0  # some poses have fewer interface contacts

scores = model(pair_feats, mask)
# Ranking loss: near-native should score higher
true_quality = torch.randn(n_poses)  # i-RMSD or DockQ scores

# Pairwise ranking loss
i_idx = torch.randint(0, n_poses, (500,))
j_idx = torch.randint(0, n_poses, (500,))
target = torch.{{b2}}(true_quality[i_idx] - true_quality[j_idx])
loss = nn.MarginRankingLoss(margin=0.1)(
    scores[i_idx], scores[j_idx], target)
loss.{{b3}}()
print(f'Ranking loss: {loss.item():.4f}')

blanks.hint: Apply softmax along the pairs dimension to compute attention weights.
Use torch.sign() to get +1 or -1 indicating which pose has higher quality.
Call backward() to backpropagate the ranking loss through the scoring function.
